fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to ship the interweave rich text renderer. Basically it needs to handle a bunch of stuff — taking user content (could be plain text, could be HTML snippets) and spitting out safe rendered HTML. Core things: wrap content in the right container tag (or just a fragment if needed), handle when people paste in HTML with dangerous stuff like scripts or onclick handlers (strip those out), normalize attributes so things like datetime and aria- and data- attributes survive but sketchy ones don't, and auto-convert line breaks in plain text to break tags (but not when disabled). Also needs to linkify content — emails, hashtags, regular URLs, and IP-based URLs should all become clickable anchors, but don't nest anchors inside existing anchors. There's also emoji stuff — matching unicode, shortcodes, emoticons and packaging emoji records with canonical shortcode/primary shortcode/skin metadata. For validation, non-string content and full HTML documents should come back with the right neutral error codes — similar to how we handled the rejection logic in that auth-input module we did before. Pattern matching for emails, hashtags, URLs, and IPs needs to expose the individual parts too. Make sure the core logic is totally decoupled from the stdin/stdout adapter layer. Refer to the existing test cases for exact output shapes.

trigger_keywords: default wrapper tag
what element wraps content when no tag is specified
fallback container for rendered output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no tagName is provided, the default outer container is a <div> element. If tagName is 'fragment', no wrapper element is emitted and only the inner rendered content is output.

trigger_keywords: line break behavior when HTML is present
newline conversion with existing HTML tags
br conversion disabled condition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Line break conversion (newline →
) only applies to plain text input. If the content already contains HTML markup (e.g., existing
tags or other HTML elements), it is not treated as plain multiline text and newlines are preserved as literal characters rather than converted. Conversion can also be explicitly disabled via options.disableLineBreaks=true.

trigger_keywords: which HTML tags are blocked
what elements are stripped during sanitization
unsafe tag removal rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tags such as <script>, <style>, <iframe>, and other non-content/executable elements are removed entirely. Event attributes (e.g., onclick, onload) are stripped from all elements. Anchor href and img src attributes with unsafe URI schemes (e.g., javascript:, xss:) are removed, leaving the element but without the unsafe attribute. Additional non-default tags may be explicitly allowed via options.

trigger_keywords: how are boolean attributes rendered
colspan and rowspan rendering
td table cell attribute output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Table cell elements like are rendered without their containing element if no valid parent context exists — in the test case, Cell renders as just 'Cell' (the text node only, no td tag). Boolean attributes like disabled='disabled' are normalized to disabled=''. Numeric attributes like colspan and rowspan are dropped when the parent structural context is absent.

trigger_keywords: datetime attribute casing
attribute name normalization rules
camelCase vs lowercase attribute output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The datetime attribute on <time> elements is output as dateTime (camelCase) in the rendered HTML, following React-style attribute normalization. All other standard attributes follow their normalized output form per the allowed attribute list.

trigger_keywords: invalid content type error code
what error is returned for non-string input
numeric content rejection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the content field is not a string (e.g., a number like 123), the system returns the error string 'error=invalid_content_type' followed by a newline. This applies to the parse_content operation.

trigger_keywords: full document rejection error
what error fires for DOCTYPE or html tags
unsupported document detection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the content contains document-level constructs such as <!DOCTYPE>, <html>, <head>, or <body> tags, the validate_document_fragment operation returns 'error=unsupported_document' followed by a newline. Plain text that merely contains bracket-like words resembling those tags is not rejected.

trigger_keywords: email pattern parts breakdown
what parts are exposed for a matched email
local part and host part extraction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a matched email, the output is: matched=true, part0=<full token>, part1=<local part before @>, part2=<host part after @>, each on its own line with a trailing newline. For example, 'user@domain.com' yields part1=user and part2=domain.com.

trigger_keywords: hashtag parts output format
what is extracted from a hashtag match
hashtag without leading hash symbol
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a matched hashtag, the output is: matched=true, part0=<full token including #>, part1=<tag text without the # marker>, each on its own line. Supported characters in the tag body include letters, numbers, underscores, and dashes. Tags consisting only of separators or containing spaces do not match.

trigger_keywords: URL part fields order
what are the 8 parts emitted for a URL match
scheme auth host port path query fragment order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a matched URL (both hostname and IP), eight parts are emitted after part0 (full token): part1=scheme (e.g., 'http://'), part2=auth, part3=host, part4=port, part5=path, part6=query (includes leading ?), part7=fragment (includes leading #). Empty fields are emitted as empty strings. URLs without a scheme match but emit part1 as empty and default the scheme during rendering.

trigger_keywords: which URLs are rejected by the url matcher
localhost and raw IP in hostname URL detection
unsupported protocols for host URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The hostname URL matcher (kind=url) rejects: localhost, raw IPv4 addresses, unsupported protocols (anything other than http/https or schemeless), and malformed hosts. Raw IPv4 addresses must be matched using kind=ip instead. The IP matcher rejects IPv6 bracket notation and out-of-range octets (>255).

trigger_keywords: autolink anchor rel attribute
what rel value is set on generated anchors
noopener noreferrer on autolinked URLs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Anchors generated by the autolinking renderer include rel="noopener noreferrer" on all generated links. Existing anchors in the input HTML are preserved as-is without modification to their rel or href attributes. Tokens already inside an existing <a> element are not wrapped in a second anchor.

trigger_keywords: punctuation trimming around autolinks
parentheses around a URL in autolinking
trailing punctuation excluded from link
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During autolinking, surrounding punctuation such as parentheses is kept outside the generated anchor. For example, '(http://domain.com/path)' renders as '(http://domain.com/path)' with the parens outside the anchor element.

trigger_keywords: emoji match output format
what JSON fields are in emoji_match response
hexcode match unicode shortcode output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The emoji_match operation returns a single-line JSON object followed by a newline. For unicode conversion (convertUnicode=true), the fields are: hexcode, match, unicode. For shortcode conversion (convertShortcode=true), the fields are: hexcode, match, shortcode. Fields are output in alphabetical key order.

trigger_keywords: package_emoji output fields
emoji packaging canonical shortcode format
skins field and primary_shortcode in packaged emoji
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The package_emoji operation enriches a raw emoji record and returns a JSON object with these additional/transformed fields: canonical_shortcodes (array of shortcodes wrapped in colons, e.g., [':cat:']), primary_shortcode (first canonical shortcode, e.g., ':cat:'), unicode (the emoji character itself), and skins (empty array if no skin variants). The output is the full merged object sorted by key, followed by a newline.

trigger_keywords: style attribute safety
which CSS declarations are stripped
url() and image functions in style attributes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Style attribute values containing CSS functions that reference external resources or code — such as url(), image(), expression() — are removed from the style declaration. Safe CSS property declarations (e.g., color, font-size) are preserved. The style attribute is omitted entirely if all its declarations are unsafe.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the rejection logic in that auth-input module we did before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 input validation logic: parse_content returns 'error=invalid_content_type' for non-string content, and validate_document_fragment returns 'error=unsupported_document' for full HTML documents containing <!DOCTYPE>, <html>, <head>, or <body> tags — see rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature5_input_validation.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the existing test cases for exact output shapes
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (feature1 through feature12) define the exact stdin/stdout contract for the execution adapter, including precise field ordering, newline placement, attribute casing (e.g., dateTime), and JSON key ordering (alphabetical) in emoji responses

context_pointers.vague_reference: make sure the core logic is totally decoupled from the stdin/stdout adapter layer
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture constraint: the execution adapter reads JSON from stdin and writes to stdout, but the core domain objects (markup renderer, pattern matchers, emoji packager) must be implemented as independent modules/classes callable without any I/O context — per the Separation of Concerns and Dependency Inversion requirements in start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: packaging emoji records with canonical shortcode/primary shortcode/skin metadata
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 12 (feature12_emoji_data_packaging.json): the package_emoji operation adds canonical_shortcodes (shortcodes array with colon-wrapped values), primary_shortcode (first canonical), unicode (emoji character), and skins (empty array default) to the raw emoji input object

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output shapes, and refer to start.md for the architectural constraints. If you can describe the specific behavior you're unsure about in business terms, I can try to clarify the intent.

feature10_integrated_autolinking.json.description: Render plain text with email, hashtag, host-name URL, and IPv4 URL tokens converted into anchor elements without nesting anchors inside existing anchors.

input.content: - https://127.0.0.1/foo - www.domain.com - (http://domain.com/some/path?with=query) - This text should stay

cases.expected_output: - https://127.0.0.1/foo - www.domain.com - (http://domain.com/some/path?with=query) - This text should stay

feature11_emoji_detection_and_rendering..description: Detect configured emoji unicode, shortcode, and emoticon tokens and render matched emoji either as unicode or image markup.

feature12_emoji_data_packaging.json.description: Package raw emoji records into enriched emoji data with canonical shortcode, primary shortcode, unicode value, and skin metadata.

feature1_markup_wrapping.json.description: Render text or empty content through a safe markup renderer using the requested outer container behavior.

feature2_line_break_conversion.json.description: Convert plain-text line breaks into rendered break elements only when the input is plain text and conversion is enabled.

feature3_safe_html_sanitization.json.description: Render supported HTML fragments while removing disallowed elements and unsafe source attributes.

feature4_attribute_normalization.json.description: Render allowed HTML attributes in normalized form and reject unsafe or unsupported attributes.

input.content:
JanCell
X

feature5_input_validation.json.description: Reject unsupported whole-document markup and non-text content with language-neutral error categories.

feature6_email_detection.json.description: Detect supported email-address tokens and expose their local and host parts.

feature7_hashtag_detection.json.description: Detect supported hashtag tokens and expose the tag text without the leading marker.

feature8_url_detection.json.description: Detect supported web URL tokens and expose their scheme, host, path, query, and fragment parts.
